Because this is well over 20 years old with no comparable current activity, Chelsea is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

name: Burnley
political_explanation: Burnley's hooligan group, Suicide Squad, organized the 'White Lives Matter Burnley' banner flown over Manchester City's stadium in 2020, in response to players kneeling for Black Lives Matter. The group included a named member of the English Defence League. Because this happened in 2020, well within the last 20 years, Burnley is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: West Ham United
political_explanation: While West Ham hasn't been directly associated with far-right activity in recent years, a hooligan firm associated with the club in the 1970s and 1980s, the Inter City Firm, has a long and well-documented history of far-right and National Front connections, including firsthand accounts from former members. Due to this historical pattern of engagement, West Ham United is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

Due to this historical pattern of engagement, Millwall is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

name: Celtic
political_explanation: Celtic's fan group, Green Brigade, has an openly socialist and Irish-republican identity, including displaying communist imagery and organizing Palestinian-solidarity actions that have drawn fines from UEFA. Because Green Brigade remains Celtic's main, highly visible group today, Celtic is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Rangers
political_explanation: Rangers' main fan group, Union Bears, is described in football journalism as pro-monarchy and loyalist, tied to the Ulster loyalist and unionist tradition among some Rangers fans historically. Because this is an ongoing identity, Rangers is considered a club with a current right-wing fan base.

name: St Johnstone
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: St Mirren
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Bayern Munich
political_explanation: Bayern's ultras group, Schickeria Munchen, runs an ongoing campaign honoring Kurt Landauer, the club's Jewish president before World War II who was persecuted by the Nazis. The club itself has publicly acknowledged this campaign. Because it is an active, ongoing project, Bayern Munich is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Bayer Leverkusen
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Eintracht Frankfurt
political_explanation: Eintracht Frankfurt's ultras group, Ultras Frankfurt '97, is widely cited as one of German football's most explicitly anti-racist groups, running ongoing anti-discrimination campaigns. Because these campaigns are active, Eintracht Frankfurt is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Borussia Dortmund
political_explanation: Dortmund's main current ultras group, The Unity, has a publicly documented anti-fascist platform and has confronted far-right elements within Dortmund's own fan base. Separately, a 1980s hooligan firm called Borussenfront was well-documented as far-right, and one of its former leaders later appeared at a far-right rally in Cologne in 2014, but that firm is no longer active and is separate from Dortmund's current ultras scene. Because of The Unity's ongoing activity, Borussia Dortmund is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: SC Freiburg
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: TSG Hoffenheim
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Union Berlin
political_explanation: Union Berlin's ultras group, Wuhlesyndikat, carries on the club's anti-fascist identity, which traces back to East Berlin's working-class and dissident culture under Communist rule. This is documented in mainstream German journalism, not just ultras-specific sources, and describes an ongoing identity. Because of this, Union Berlin is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Werder Bremen
political_explanation: Werder Bremen's main current ultras groups, Infamous Youth and Sherwood Youth, are described as left-leaning in German fan culture, including a documented friendship with St. Pauli's far-left ultras. Separately, a hooligan crew called Endstufe-Crew Bremen was identified by anti-fascist monitors among the far-right crowd at a rally in Cologne in October 2014. Both things are true of the club's fan base. Because the left-leaning ultras groups are the larger and more visible presence, and both findings fall within the last 20 years, Werder Bremen is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Borussia Mönchengladbach
political_explanation: Gladbach's ultras scene, including a group called Sottocultura Ultras Gladbach, is described as right-leaning in German fan-forum discussions. This is a weaker source than most reporting on other clubs - an informal forum rather than journalism or an official finding - but it describes an ongoing reputation. Because of this, Borussia Monchengladbach is considered a club with a current right-wing fan base, though with lower confidence than other entries.

name: 1. FC Köln
political_explanation: Cologne's fan culture, including the Wilde Horde ultras group, is generally described as anti-fascist and tolerant, in keeping with the city's broader progressive reputation in Germany. This reputation is ongoing. Because of this, 1. FC Koln is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Schalke 04
political_explanation: Two Schalke fan groups, Ultras Gelsenkirchen and Marler Jungs, physically blocked a neo-Nazi rally from reaching Gelsenkirchen in May 2015 - confirmed even by the far-right party that organized the rally, which complained about the blockade. Because this happened within the last 20 years, Schalke 04 is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: SV Elversberg
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Hertha BSC
political_explanation: Hertha's organized support has a documented history of far-right hooligan activity tied to a banned hooligan network, Hertha Froesche, reported on repeatedly by German media. Unlike Dresden, Magdeburg, and Cottbus, Hertha is a West Berlin club, showing this pattern isn't limited to former East German clubs. Because this reputation is ongoing, Hertha BSC is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Energie Cottbus
political_explanation: Cottbus's hooligan group, Inferno Cottbus, has a long-running far-right reputation, regularly cited as one of the clearest post-reunification cases in Germany alongside Dresden and Magdeburg. This reputation is still actively discussed today. Because of this, Energie Cottbus is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Darmstadt 98
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Dynamo Dresden
political_explanation: Dresden's ultras group, Ultras Dynamo, is one of the most widely reported cases of organized far-right hooliganism in German football, tied to the East German neo-Nazi hooligan scene that grew after reunification. This reputation is still actively reported today. Because of this, Dynamo Dresden is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: 1. FC Magdeburg
political_explanation: Magdeburg's hooligan group, Blue Generation, has a documented history of far-right incidents reported repeatedly and recently in German media. This fits a wider pattern of far-right hooligan activity at former East German clubs, also seen at Dresden and Cottbus. Because of this ongoing reputation, 1. FC Magdeburg is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: FC St. Pauli
political_explanation: St. Pauli's anti-fascist, anti-racist identity is official club policy - the club formally bans fascist symbolism. Because this is a live, ongoing policy, FC St. Pauli is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: VfL Wolfsburg
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Paris Saint-Germain
political_explanation: PSG's ultras history was split for years between a far-right-linked end and a left-wing, multicultural end, until the club disbanded all its ultras groups in 2010 after violence. The current group, Collectif Ultras Paris, formed in 2016, has organized left-leaning solidarity actions, including raising money after the 2020 Beirut port explosion. Because this is the club's active, present-day group, PSG is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: RC Lens
political_explanation: Lens's ultras group, Red Tigers Lens 1994, is described as anti-racist by Les Utopiques, tied to the region's coal-mining, working-class history. Because this is an ongoing identity, RC Lens is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Olympique de Marseille
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Olympique Lyonnais
political_explanation: News coverage of Lyon's ultras group, Bad Gones, has extensive and consistent reports over more than a decade, up through 2026, indicating a far-right and nationalist ideology among the group. Spanish outlets have described them as 'known throughout Europe for its far-right ideology' and reported police treating them as far-right for security purposes. Due to this recent and ongoing history, Olympique Lyonnais is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: LOSC Lille
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Stade Rennais

name: RC Strasbourg
political_explanation: The French Interior Ministry, in an official circular aimed at several ultras groups, accused Strasbourg's Offenders group of being 'a neo-Nazi cell disguised as a supporters' identity.' Because this is a recent government finding, RC Strasbourg is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Toulouse FC
political_explanation: Toulouse's main ultras group, Indians Tolosa, is documented as anti-racist and officially apolitical, displaying anti-racist banners and banning French flags, which carry far-right associations in French ultras culture. However, French outlet L'Empaille reports that this approach has allowed a separate group, Camside Tolosa, to operate within the same stand, including reportedly hosting visiting neo-Nazi hooligans in recent years. Because of Camside Tolosa's continued presence, Toulouse is considered a club with a current far-right sympathizer faction within its fan base.

name: Montpellier HSC
political_explanation: Montpellier's current ultras group, Armata Ultras, describes itself as anti-fascist and multicultural. French sports-culture outlet Dialectik Football has contrasted Armata's clear position with the more evasive 'apolitical' framing used by similar groups elsewhere in the region. Because this is the club's current, active group, Montpellier is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: AS Nancy Lorraine
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Pau FC
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Red Star FC
political_explanation: Red Star's fan group, Red Star Fans, is described by French Wikipedia as anti-fascist and left-leaning, in keeping with the club's own historic identity tied to Paris's 'red suburb' political tradition. The group faced government dissolution threats as recently as 2025. Because of this, Red Star is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Stade de Reims
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: AS Saint-Étienne
political_explanation: Saint-Etienne's ultras group, Green Angels, is described as anti-fascist by French left-wing supporter-culture outlet Les Utopiques, tied to the club's mining-region, working-class roots. Because this is an ongoing identity, Saint-Etienne is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: FC Metz
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: FC Sochaux-Montbéliard
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Inter Milan
political_explanation: Reporting has tied a hardcore faction within Inter's fan base, called Boys San, to far-right political figures and, in some cases, neo-fascist-linked hooligan networks. This describes a historically significant faction rather than Inter's fan base as a whole, which carries no similar reputation. Because the clearest evidence describes a historic rather than ongoing faction, Inter Milan is considered a club with a historical far-right sympathizer faction within its fan base.

name: AC Milan
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Napoli
political_explanation: Napoli's organized support, including groups called Ultras Napoli 1972 and Fedayn EAM 1979, has a documented history of organizing against anti-Neapolitan racism - discriminatory chants that fans of northern Italian clubs have long directed at Naples. Because this organizing is ongoing, Napoli is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: AS Roma
political_explanation: Roma's ultras group Fedayn was founded in 1972 by Roberto Rulli with openly left-wing and anarchist politics, drawn from working-class Rome neighborhoods, and was set up specifically as the political opposite of Roma's other historic group, the right-wing 'Boys.' The group remains part of Roma's fan base today. Because of this, AS Roma is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Juventus
political_explanation: Juventus's ultras group, Drughi, has been tied to repeated, documented racist incidents - specifically monkey-chanting aimed at Black opposing players - across multiple recent seasons. Because this pattern is ongoing, Juventus is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Como
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Atalanta
political_explanation: Atalanta's ultras group, Wild Boys, tracing back to a 1980s group called Wild Kaos, is documented as right-leaning with reported ties to the regional right-wing party Lega Lombarda. This is separate from an older, unrelated left-wing group at the same club, Brigate Nerazzurre, from the 1970s. Because Wild Boys remains an active named group today, Atalanta is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Bologna
political_explanation: Bologna's ultras group, Curva Andrea Costa, has a documented left-wing, anti-fascist reputation, fitting with Emilia-Romagna's history as one of Italy's most left-leaning regions. This reputation is ongoing. Because of this, Bologna is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Lazio
political_explanation: Lazio's ultras group, Irriducibili, is one of the most widely reported far-right ultras groups in European football, with a long and ongoing history of fascist and neo-Nazi symbolism, including pro-Mussolini banners and antisemitic chants. Because this reputation remains active, Lazio is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Udinese
political_explanation: Udinese's ultras group, Hooligans Teddy Boys, has a documented history of right-wing and racist actions, most notably a specific, widely-reported antisemitic incident in 1989 aimed at a new signing. Because the clearest documented evidence dates back more than 20 years, Udinese is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

name: Torino
political_explanation: Italian political journalism (Orizzonti Politici) describes Ultras Torino as the more moderate, left-leaning faction within Torino's fan base, alongside a separate, opposing far-right splinter group in the same stand called Torino Hooligans. Because this split is ongoing, Torino is considered a club with a current left-leaning faction within its fan base.

name: Sassuolo
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Parma
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Cagliari
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Genoa
political_explanation: Genoa's ultras group, Gradinata Nord, has a documented right-wing, nationalist reputation, often contrasted with crosstown rival Sampdoria's left-leaning fan base. Because this reputation is ongoing, Genoa is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Lecce
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Fiorentina
political_explanation: Fiorentina's ultras group, Collettivo Autonomo Viola, takes its name from Italy's 'collettivo autonomo' tradition, specific far-left political language, and the group still organizes around anti-fascist politics today. Because of this ongoing activity, Fiorentina is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Venezia
political_explanation: Venezia's ultras group, Curva Sud Venezia, has displayed anti-fascist banners and messaging. Because this is an ongoing identity, Venezia is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Frosinone

name: Cesena

name: Sudtirol
political_explanation: Sudtirol's ultras group, Gradinata Nord Bolzano, formed around 2020, is directly described by Italian Wikipedia's own article as far-right, tied to an ethnic-Italian identity in a region where German speakers are the majority. Because the group formed recently and remains active, Sudtirol is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Carrarese
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Empoli
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Sampdoria
political_explanation: Sampdoria's ultras group, Ultras Tito Cucchiaroni, has a left-leaning identity tied to Genoa's working-class port history, often contrasted with crosstown rival Genoa's more right-leaning fan base. Because this identity is ongoing, Sampdoria is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Hellas Verona
political_explanation: Verona's ultras group, Brigate Gialloblu, has repeatedly displayed swastikas and Celtic crosses at matches, and the club has been formally sanctioned by football authorities over racist chanting tied to the group. The group is still active today. Because of this, Hellas Verona is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Benevento
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Ascoli
record: 23W-8D-5L, 77 pts — Serie C 2025-26, won promotion play-off
political_explanation: Ascoli's ultras group, Settembre Bianconero, is listed among the right-leaning groups in a well-known Italian newspaper mapping of ultras politics, Corriere della Sera. The group remains one of Ascoli's principal ultras groups today. Because of this, Ascoli is considered a club with a current right-wing fan base.

name: Barcelona
political_explanation: Barcelona had an ultras group called Boixos Nois whose members had a far-right, Catalan-separatist reputation, well documented in Spanish press. The club banned the group from the Camp Nou in 2003 after years of violence and racism linked to them. Matchday atmosphere since then has come from official, club-sanctioned fan clubs with no similar reputation. Because this activity dates back more than 20 years, Barcelona is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

name: Real Madrid
political_explanation: Real Madrid's hardcore fan group, Ultra Sur, has a long-documented far-right reputation with ties to Francoism. The club banned them from the Bernabeu in 2013-2014 after years of violence and racism. The group has remained in the news since through an ongoing public feud with club president Florentino Perez. Because that conflict is still active, Real Madrid is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Villarreal
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Atletico Madrid
political_explanation: Atletico's Frente Atletico is one of the most widely reported far-right ultras groups in European football, with a peak membership around 2,500. In 1998, members of a section of the group called 'Bastion' killed a rival Real Sociedad fan, Aitor Zabaleta, outside the old stadium. Frente Atletico remains active and central to Atletico's matchday support today. Because of this, Atletico Madrid is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Real Betis
political_explanation: Betis's main ultras group, Supporters Gol Sur, has been directly named as far-right-linked in Spanish investigative reporting on football's connection to far-right street movements. The group remains active and central to matchday atmosphere at the Benito Villamarin. Because of this, Real Betis is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Celta Vigo
political_explanation: Celta's main ultras group, Celtarras, has a left-leaning, Galician-regionalist identity documented in Spanish football reporting. The group remains Celta's main organized support today. Because of this, Celta Vigo is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Getafe
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Rayo Vallecano
political_explanation: Rayo's ultras group, Bukaneros, openly calls itself the 'symbol of the working class' and ties its identity to Vallecas, a historically left-wing, working-class part of Madrid. The group has campaigned against signing a player over his reported far-right sympathies, and has been active continuously since 1992. Because of this, Rayo Vallecano is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Valencia
political_explanation: Valencia's main ultras group, Ultras Yomos, has a long-running right-wing and nationalist reputation in Spanish football reporting. A former leader of the group has also publicly identified himself as far-right, reportedly using Falangist salutes and slogans. The group remains Valencia's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Valencia is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Real Sociedad
political_explanation: Real Sociedad's ultras group, Herri Norte Taldea, has a left-wing Basque nationalist identity tied to the Basque independence movement. This is a well-established, decades-old identity, and the group remains the club's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Real Sociedad is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Espanyol
political_explanation: Espanyol had an ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiazules, named as far-right in Spanish reporting on the football roots of far-right street movements. The group was officially disbanded in 2010. Matchday atmosphere today comes from two newer groups, La Curva and Juvenil 1991, which carry no similar political reputation. Because the far-right period ended over a decade ago, Espanyol is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

name: Athletic Bilbao
political_explanation: Athletic's ultras group, Herri Norte, shares the same left-wing Basque nationalist identity found at Real Sociedad. The club reinforces this by only signing Basque-raised players, a policy that ties Basque identity politics to the club itself, not just its fans. The group has been active since 1982 and remains Athletic's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Athletic Bilbao is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Sevilla
political_explanation: Sevilla's ultras group, Biris Norte, has a long-running left-wing reputation and is considered one of the first organized ultras groups in Spanish football, founded in 1975. It remains Sevilla's main ultras group today, in direct contrast to city rival Real Betis's far-right-linked group. Because of this, Sevilla is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Alaves
political_explanation: Alaves's ultras group, Indar Gorri (Basque for 'Red Force'), shares the left-wing Basque nationalist identity documented at Osasuna, Athletic Bilbao, and Real Sociedad. The group remains Alaves's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Alaves is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Elche
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Levante
political_explanation: Levante's ultras group, Frente Bukanero, has a documented left-wing, anti-fascist identity, often described in direct contrast to city rival Valencia's right-wing Ultras Yomos. It remains Levante's main ultras group today. Because of this, Levante is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Osasuna
political_explanation: Osasuna's ultras group, Indar Gorri, is described in Spanish football reporting using the term 'abertzale' - Basque and Navarrese left-nationalism, the same political tradition found at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, and Alaves. The group has been active since 1987 and remains Osasuna's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Osasuna is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Racing Santander
political_explanation: Racing's main ultras group, Juventudes Verdiblancas, has been active since the 1980s. The Spanish ultras site Mundo Ultras reports that its public image has been 'associated in different periods with the far right.' The group remains Racing's central and active ultras presence today. Because of this, Racing Santander is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Deportivo La Coruna
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Malaga
political_explanation: Malaga's ultras group, Frente Bokeron, is directly described as extreme-right in Spanish football reporting. The group remains Malaga's main and active ultras presence today. Because of this, Malaga is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Castellon
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Las Palmas
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Cordoba
political_explanation: Reports on Cordoba's ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiverdes, disagree on some details - one Spanish ultras site calls the group apolitical, while a more detailed fan forum says plainly that 'politically they are nationalist,' using that term the same way it describes other right-leaning Spanish groups. Because the weight of reporting leans toward a right-wing, nationalist reputation, Cordoba is considered a club with a current right-wing fan base.

name: Sporting Gijon
political_explanation: Sporting Gijon's ultras group, Ultra Boys, is directly described as extreme-right in Spanish football reporting, with a documented history of physical fights. The group remains active today. Because of this, Sporting Gijon is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Eibar
political_explanation: Eibar's ultras group, Eskozia la Brava, is described in Spanish football reporting as far-left and 'abertzale' - the same Basque left-nationalist tradition documented at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, Osasuna, and Alaves. The group remains Eibar's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Eibar is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Cadiz
political_explanation: Cadiz's ultras group, Brigada Amarilla, is one of Spain's oldest self-identified left-wing ultras groups, with documented friendships with other left-wing Spanish groups like Rayo's Bukaneros. It remains Cadiz's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Cadiz is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Albacete
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Real Sociedad B
political_explanation: This is a reserve team that shares the same fan base as Real Sociedad. Real Sociedad's ultras group, Herri Norte Taldea, has a left-wing Basque nationalist identity tied to the Basque independence movement. This is a well-established, decades-old identity, and the group remains the club's main ultras presence today. Because of this, Real Sociedad is considered a club with a current left-wing fan base.

name: Leganes

name: Real Valladolid
political_explanation: Valladolid's ultras scene split into two rival groups in 1996: Ultras Violetas, described as far-right, and Fossa Garrafoni, an explicitly far-left splinter. Both groups have since been dissolved. Because this political split happened almost 30 years ago and neither group is active today, Real Valladolid is considered a club with a historical far-right fan base.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: ADO Den Haag
political_explanation: ADO Den Haag's hardcore support group, North Side, has a history of racist chanting incidents. In September 2025, a banner from ADO's own stadium was spotted among rioters at a far-right riot in The Hague, where people made Nazi salutes alongside Defend Netherlands, a group formally classified as far-right by Dutch intelligence. Because of this direct and recent involvement, ADO Den Haag is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Ajax
political_explanation: Before the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, Ajax's hardcore fan group, F-Side, said politics and football should be kept separate, while also saying they would physically stop a planned pro-Palestinian protest near the stadium. Moving to block one side of a political demonstration is itself a political act. Because this happened in 2024, Ajax is considered a club with a current right-wing-leaning fan base.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Feyenoord
political_explanation: Feyenoord's hardcore support section, Vak S, has a documented history of far-right-linked hooliganism and antisemitic chanting directed at rival Ajax. This is an ongoing pattern in the Ajax-Feyenoord rivalry. Because of this, Feyenoord is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: SC Cambuur
political_explanation: Hooligan groups associated with SC Cambuur have a history of far-right engagement. One hooligan firm connected to the club was specifically involved in a far-right riot in The Hague in September 2025. Rioters at that demonstration posed under a banner reading 'Dutch Firms United' alongside Defend Netherlands, which Dutch intelligence has formally classified as far-right. Because of the direct and recent nature of this involvement, SC Cambuur is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Jong Ajax
political_explanation: This is a reserve team that shares the same fan base as Ajax. Before the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, Ajax's hardcore fan group, F-Side, said politics and football should be kept separate, while also saying they would physically stop a planned pro-Palestinian protest near the stadium. Moving to block one side of a political demonstration is itself a political act. Because this happened in 2024, Ajax is considered a club with a current right-wing-leaning fan base.

name: NAC Breda
political_explanation: Hooligan groups associated with NAC Breda have a history of far-right engagement. A hooligan firm connected to the club was specifically involved in the same September 2025 far-right riot in The Hague as SC Cambuur and ADO Den Haag, posing under a 'Dutch Firms United' banner alongside Defend Netherlands, a group Dutch intelligence has formally classified as far-right. Because of the direct and recent nature of this involvement, NAC Breda is considered a club with a current far-right fan base.

name: Heracles Almelo
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: FC Porto
political_explanation: Portuguese sports outlet JPN has reported on a far-right faction, called Divisao Azul, operating within Porto's main fan group, Super Dragoes, while noting that Super Dragoes itself doesn't publicly claim any political position. Because this describes an ongoing situation, FC Porto is considered a club with a current far-right sympathizer faction within its fan base.

name: Sporting CP
political_explanation: Sporting's older fan group, Juventude Leonina, also called Juve Leo, included a far-right, neo-Nazi faction called Grupo 1143, led by a figure connected to Hammerskins Portugal, documented by Portuguese newspapers. That faction was reported inactive by 2015, and Sporting's current official fan group, Brigada Ultras Sporting, has no similar finding attached to it. Because the clearest evidence describes a now-inactive faction, Sporting CP is considered a club with a historical far-right sympathizer faction within its fan base.

name: SL Benfica
political_explanation: A member of Benfica's fan group, No Name Boys, told an interviewer that swastika flags have been displayed within their section of the stadium. The same member denied the group as a whole has a formal ideology, and a Portuguese police intelligence report from the mid-2000s described far-right elements existing somewhere within Benfica's fan scene generally, without pinning it on one specific group. Because this describes an ongoing situation, Benfica is considered a club with a current far-right sympathizer faction within its fan base.
